content.problemStatement:
You've learned how to clone a GitHub repository. \
Cloning works great when:
- the repo belongs to you, or
- you have permission to push to it. 
But now imagine this situation:

---

You find an amazing open-source project on GitHub: \
**“Recipe-Collection”** created by another developer. 

You want to: **add a new recipe** \
So you think: \
“Let me clone it and push my changes!”

**You clone it:**
```aaa
git clone https://github.com/someone/recipe-collection.git
```
You make your edits and **then try to push:**
```sss
git push
```
**And GitHub says:**

❌ **ERROR: Permission denied. You cannot push to this repository**.

Because… \
You are not the owner. You **don’t have write access**.

---

**This is where Forking comes in** \
When you don’t have permission to push to a repo, you create your own copy of it on your GitHub account.

Meaning:
- You get **full control over your fork**.
- You can **push your changes freely**.
- Later, you can **send a pull request to the owner**.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
